# Transcrição: 02-Uncovering Opportunities via Audience Insights

## [00:00 - 03:01]

For this part of building up my audiences, if I don't have data, so if I've got no lookalike, no custom data, for example, to run on, I will go into audience insights. Sometimes I do this anyway because it's really useful to get an insight into potential ideas for interest targeting, but also going deeper into your page. To give you an example, when you start off an ad account and you have no data and you start off with interest and you start to build up fans on your page and you start to spend more and more money and those hundreds of fans become a thousands of fans and they become tens of thousands of fans, the data when you pull it back into audience insights is really, really interesting. So there's many ways you can break out audience insights and FYI, it's in business manager, it's in your mega menu. Now when you go into audience insights, it defaults into US, I think that's kind of just the default wherever you are. At the moment, you can see there's 150 to 200 million people in this audience. So all that's been selected here is United States. You can break it down then by age, gender, interests, connections. So for example, that is pages and then some advanced breakdowns here. If you look at the data here, so this is based on just everyone in the US. So it gives you an idea of kind of what the breakdowns are by age, relationship status, education level, job titles, etc. You can then look at the top page likes for this particular group of people which are everyone, which is everyone in the US for example, you can see the top categories, then which brands represent the top categories and you can scroll all the way through and get that. Then you get the page likes. So these are top categories and then the pages link to those. And then these are the pages and then the number of people in that audience as well. So if we list it by size, which it doesn't always do by default. So affinity is the default, the sorting in terms of relevance. If we do it just simply by size, Walmart is at 22.3 million page likes, the biggest page in the US followed by Amazon.com, Target, etc. So that's all interesting. Then you can split it by location and it's just showing the percentage of people in different areas. So not too much going on here, top countries just US. Top languages quite interesting. US Spanish is quite common because it's so close to Mexico for example. So nothing mind blowing at the moment. You look at activity, it shows you on average how many ads are clicked, promotions deemed and there's a few different breakdowns. So that's interesting. Now let's say we have our 5W avatar and we're in the gifting space and we're into people

## [03:01 - 06:01]

that are gifting jewelry for example. So we come along and we type in jewelry. So now we're in the US and we've narrowed it down by people who are interested in Europe in jewelry. So that was initially 150 to 200 million people. Just by typing in jewelry, we've cut that down to 70 to 80 million people, still a lot of people. But now we get some interesting data to look at the indexes of how people that are interested in jewelry compared to the whole of the US. So the gray bars here represent our baseline, which are the whole of the US and the blue bars are now the people that are interested in jewelry. So here for example, we see that 25 to 34 are the most linked to jewelry as an interest. It's just important to say it's not people that are most likely to purchase or anything like that. This is just saying that they have jewelry as an interest. So 25 to 34 and that kind of makes sense. There's a big gifting part of jewelry at this age as well. And it says most is women and less is men, but interestingly against the index. So 45% of all Facebook users in the US are men, but only 29% have an interest that comes up against their profile of jewelry. Interesting. So if we're targeting jewelry, we'll probably have more success. We'll have more success in women potentially because there's more of them that have that interest, but maybe it costs a cheaper for men. So it's just a few things that I'd be balancing out. Then you kind of look down here. There's not too much interesting stuff going on here. Then you kind of look further down and look at their job titles. For whatever reason, there's an over indexing of people that are in the healthcare and medical services that are interested in jewelry, in sales, in administrative services. Just gives you an idea of kind of what I'm doing here. I hope you're taking this away. I'm slowly building up the profile of my ideal prospect. So I'm just digging deeper, digging deeper. And what I would be doing at this stage is just writing down notes. So I would be making note of the fact that there's more women that show an interest, certain job titles are coming up. Then I go into page likes and it starts to get really interesting here. So these are the top categories where the interest is related to jewelry. So U, La, Lama, the Mint, Julep, Boutique, one country, and then this split by category and kind of different things are going on. Now, what the cool thing about this is what Facebook are doing is taking categories, is taking people's profiles and then the people that are related to the pages to give you an idea of all the kind of cross affinities that are going on.

## [06:01 - 09:03]

Why this is useful is if you're in the jewelry space, for example, and you want to find new pockets of audiences, I would be looking at these kind of things. So here's a great example. People that are fans of Groupon are also interested in jewelry. So if I was to target Groupon as an interest, then I would potentially find people who are, and thinking about Groupon as a product that are after discounts and things like that, that are also interested in jewelry. So if I had a jewelry brand and it was discount based, I'd be targeting Groupon as an interest, for example, and you can kind of go down further down and you get more kind of breakdowns of different ideas that you can cover in this. Product and services, Fabletics, Covergirl, XInfinity, other ideas for you to look at, other pages that may be related to jewelry, for example. So here's one here. So this is actually a deals based page, for example, 2.6 million fans. It's huge. So if you start to scroll down, it doesn't seem to be too much jewelry stuff going on, but that just gives you an idea in terms of affinity that a lot of people that are fans of this particular page have an interest in jewelry. If, for example, you wanted to target people that are interested in discounted jewelry, then targeting this page and what I mean by that is if you copy the page name, what you can do is to see if that comes up as an interest. It will only come up as an interest if it has, I believe, there is a threshold and I'm not sure exactly what the number is, but generally, if there's a certain number of interests related, then the page does come up. This is this quite interestingly doesn't come up as an interest, which is a bit of a shame, considering the number of fans it has. If I look at this one here, so Covergirl 4.6 million. Let's let's try Covergirl and see if that comes up as an interest. So there we go. Covergirl does come up. Now let's have a look at the profile of a Covergirl fan. So there's 2.3 to 3 million fans, probably obviously 92% of women. Majority are actually over indexing 18 to 24, 25 to 34. So this is really like the bulk of the audience is really 18 to 34, maybe up to 44. Generally, not married, the majority, their inner relationship is single. So there's just a few things I'm pulling out here to start building additional profiles. And I'll just come back to kind of why. Again, I can now look at interests related to Covergirl based on my avatar of someone

## [09:03 - 12:06]

that's interested in jewelry and start to go deeper and deeper down that rabbit hole. And then I might look at what kind of things a Covergirl fan is interested in. So Covergirl here, if I look at the about, let's have a look, they don't have an about. So I don't personally know this brand. I can have a look at their website and get an idea of what they're about. So health and beauty. Is it just lipstick, a new fresh collection, etc., etc. So mascara, lip color, etc. So I can build up a profile of this person that they want to look great. So if that's the context and that's part of my avatar, if I go back to my 5W avatar, and I'm selling jewelry, for example, and I want to target Covergirls and interest, then my add angle is all about looking great. I just want to make sure you're connecting the dots here as I'm going through my research and this is literally on the fly. Like I'm not in the market to sell jewelry to women that want to look great. But if I was, then I'm starting to build this profile up. And the cool thing about this is if I'm selling jewelry to someone that's a Covergirl fan, because I know I can target them, it's come up here. I know this profile already. But the other cool thing about this is when you do find an interest in this related to a page, come down into page likes and it doesn't need to be a page that could be just an interest. But the cool thing about this is it gives you an idea of other pages that are related to this as well. And that come close to your profile. So one of the cool things about this is Facebook showed the affinity based on the audience in your kind of research here and the total audience that they have on a monthly basis, for example. So the affinity is what drives the relevance. But what I find more interesting is listing it by audience size and looking at the biggest audiences. So this says, for example, in this profile of Covergirl in the US, 736 K people or 3.6. But also Macy's has 360 K. Now, the reason why relevance is 71 is because it's a smaller proportion of the total fans. But that's still a huge number of your potential prospects that are also Macy's fans as well. That are also Victoria's Secret Pink, but about Bath and Body Works, Coles, Sephora. You can kind of start to build up a profile of who this person is.

## [12:06 - 15:14]

Chances are they are 18 to 25. They like to look great. They like to buy nice things. Probably got expendable income. So I would start building up my list of interests based on these ones here. Now, not all of them will be available for you to search, but I'm pretty sure the majority will. And the way that you do your search is you can take Sephora, for example, if I want to search that, I would type it here. And see if it comes up. Yep, it comes up. So that's the one I can target. Let's try Cole. Cole's comes up. That's great. Macy's. Yeah, so the majority of these because of the fan sizes are targetable. And they're they're now building my list up. So going back to the previous part of this training, if I have no data to start with, this is where I'm coming. And I'm building up my profile as a go. I'm building up my list of interests. But but also if even if I do have audiences, if I need to build new ones, I can start doing it this way as well. And one of the other cool things about building lookalikes is, for example, if I run an ad campaign, if I run some ad sets, specifically on this profile of customer, so someone who's a fan of cover girls that likes Sephora, just fab forever 21. So if I go after that person 18 to 25, I know they're quite sassy. They've got money to spend. They will look great, etc. I can run that ad set to a dedicated landing page that's separate to everything else. So I know that the traffic going to that landing page is only this particular profile. If that profile is profitable, i.e. I'm getting a good cost practice issue and or return on ad spend or whatever I want to get out of it. I can create a lookalike of those and expand that out. And I just want you to understand the power of this here. If people land on that landing page and a lot of people convert, I can now run lookalikes on, you know, 1% of people that landed on that landing page. I could, you know, if you're getting quite technical, tag the people through that journey and install something in my back end database to know that they came through that landing page and also create a lookalike of people that purchase so that I know that these people are the ones that came through this particular interest, this particular landing page. And I can create lookalikes from it. So there's so many ways you can slice your data in to really quick, quick, high quality lookalike data and kind of expand your audiences out. So if you get 1% and that works well, you know, test the 3%, the 5% all the way up to 10 and potentially 20%. And that's all just done through a few minutes of quick analysis at the interest level. This is something which you can spend easily 30, 40, 60 minutes going quite deep covering up lots of different interests going and splitting by age and gender, looking at different countries, etc.

## [15:14 - 17:06]

So, you know, there's a huge amount of potential that can be created from here. And also, if you have a page, you can go into your pages that you are connected to. So pages you own or you've got access to. You can select the page here and you get the full demographic breakdown of who you're targeting there as one. And that's another cool play where, for example, if you have developed a lot of page likes over time, or if you're an advertiser for another business, freelancer or agency, and you've just taken on a client or you're doing an audit, you can actually come here and find out the profile of their customer, which a client probably doesn't even know. And you can give them great insights as to kind of who their customer is. So audience insights has a lot of data that you can use back in the day when things were a bit freer. You can actually upload email lists. You can't do that right now. Maybe it'll come back in the future. But the other thing you can also do is you can open custom audiences from the top here and look at those in a bit more detail. You can also do some research and save those audiences as well. And you can create ads right from here as well. So there are other features that will always be kind of reviewed and bolted on. Audience insights doesn't get a lot of attention from Facebook and get talked about a lot within groups and forums. But it is a huge area of research. So I would encourage you to play around with this, have a look at interests, go deeper into your avatar, load up your pages, play around with some of the other settings. Like for me to go deep into everything, it would probably bore you and also take up way too much time. But there's so many ways you can split this out to have a look at your audiences and then, you know, just push those into your testing to see what kind of performance you can get out of it as well.
